rationale: The citing paper uses Kivetz, Netzer, and Srinivasan (2004a) as a central prior model in its theory-building section, describing it as a key empirical context-dependent model for the compromise effect. It repeatedly positions its own contribution against that work by arguing that prior models (including Kivetz et al. 2004a) capture at most one context effect, while the new model jointly captures compromise, attraction, and similarity effects. The literature review table also explicitly places Kivetz et al. as an antecedent model class that the current study advances beyond, indicating a meaningful framing and contribution role rather than a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Kivetz et al. (2004a) as prior compromise-effect choice modeling and extends that line by proposing and testing a unified empirical model that simultaneously incorporates compromise, attraction, and similarity effects (plus their interaction). Thus, the cited paper functions as a direct stepping stone for the citing paper's expanded context-effects modeling contribution.
